tweets.text:
As you may have heard or read, I have argued for a while that the Federal Reserve could learn a thing or two from other central banks, particularly the Bank of England. (Some examples include external members on the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) to counter groupthink, the use of scenario analyses, and the publication of the inflation report to provide better, rather than just more, information.)
In this regard, it is encouraging that the new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, has tapped Mervyn King, the former Governor of the Bank of England, to co-chair one of the reform task forces.
#economy #markets #centralbanks #federalreserve @bankofengland

(Some examples include external members on the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) to counter groupthink, the use of scenario analyses, and the publication of the inflation report to provide better, rather than just more, information.)
In this regard, it is encouraging that the new Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, has tapped Mervyn King, the former Governor of the Bank of England, to co-chair one of the reform task forces.

They can suppress crude prices all they want, but at the end of the day, consumers use products. And in this case, gasoline prices have to go up to price OUT exports. If it doesn't, US won't have the storage buffers it needs.

And since the US will have to push prices up to price out exports, the rest of the world will need more gasoline, so unless China decides to lift export bans and start pumping out 2.5 million b/d of gasoline via exports, you just hit the wall.

The growing divergence between refining margins and crude prices means that, at this point, it doesn't matter what your product yield is; you just buy crude because you are going to make money. We are headed there.

tweets.text:
One of my favorite lines from Gavin is "if you're going to do a hard thing, do the whole thing."

Etched has done some genuinely impressive things to vertically integrate.

They own a factory in Taiwan. Built a two-megawatt data center in their office and cloned test stations for 24/7 cycles across both sites.

"We want to vertically integrate the entire product as much as possible, both because we get more performance and because we can move way faster.

We're the only startup right now building its own rack as well as its own chips, and we did it all at the same time.

We went through multiple iterations of the rack before the chips even came back.

We made thermal chips with the exact same hotspots we expected, so we could build the cold plates, over-pressurize them, and blow them up.

We haven't had a single leak since our real chips came back, because we'd already validated everything.

We have a factory in Taiwan. We built a clone of a bunch of the test stations in our office.

We have a two-megawatt data center on this floor. We did 24/7 development cycles.

It's that extreme vertical integration and extreme parallelization of the schedule that lets you get products to market way faster."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Three years ago, two Harvard dropouts set out to build a better AI chip than the largest companies in the world.

"Equity holders have legal protections, token holders have "trust me bro we'll keep buying and burning".

Equity holders hold the majority of tokens, and the token is the largest asset on the balance sheet. The incentives in our case are aligned. 

"I think it's a difficult argument to make that the best use of that revenue stream is to buy and burn a token rather than pay it out as dividends."

The best use of the revenue stream is whatever brings shareholders the most value. This was true before the raise, and is true after.

So why would Venice buy and burn the token post raise?  Same reason it choose to start doing so before the raise... because Venice holds more of it than anyone else. 

We are not burning to be altruistic.

tweets.text:
this last year has probably been the most fun for me in any market ever 

its been very exciting to have to deep dive into new and upcoming technologies that are going to have some of the most insane impacts we have ever seen and this is very broadly speaking across all sectors. both good and bad. But there is truly something for everyone because the ai trade has been all encompassing

the other side is, i got to spend so much time around some of the brightest minds in the world. traders, hedge fund managers, small family offices. and really just get to see how portfolios are built. (i touched on this a lot last September) It is always a lot of fun realizing that no matter how much you know, you know a lot less than you think you do. it was very humbling 

of course, markets going up as much as they have is fun too. but this time when i bought things, it felt real and tangible and i can only hope that the people who are really diving in are having as much fun as i am.

Different degrees of privacy based on your need in the moment, but always more private than the alternatives.

Most of Etherfi's revenue comes from its card business rather than staking.

@ether_fi Cash makes up more than 60% of revenue, and daily volume has climbed from a few hundred thousand dollars to a few million. The consumer business now outweighs the original staking protocol.

The larger vision is a single vault that holds your crypto, stocks, and commodities and lets you borrow or spend against any of it. You would no longer need to sell your holdings to free up cash.

Traditional banks don't offer this because letting you borrow against your assets would undercut their trading and lending fees. This is still a roadmap but it shows Etherfi's goal of becoming a full onchain bank.

tweets.text:
I have a position in SOL again for the first time since selling early last year. First entry is ~71. 

It's not the biggest position, but it felt like reasonable value when looking ahead 12-18 months, which is my time horizon for most spot positions here. 

I felt there was a loss of focus following the memecoin szn blow off, but Solana seems to be back on track when looking at tech improvements (alpenglow), tokenomics (inflation reduction), metrics (ATH in transaction activity) and a focus on spot and RWA trading. 

I know there is the comparison with ETH last cycle, but I don't think any of the same issues Ethereum was facing (scaling, foundation complacency, outsourcing of execution to L2s etc.) apply to Solana, so it feels like a lazy analogy. Devils advocate is the BTC pair, which does resemble ETH/BTC. 

I will keep adding over the coming months and hopefully end up with a decent bear market average.

I still think there is another low to come for BTC in the coming months, but possible that SOL rallies first and then doesn't make a lower low when it gets pegged back before we exit the bear. 

I don't know if it makes a new ATH, as that will depend on a lot more adoption and breakout apps, but I certainly think it can go back up a fair bit.

There’s a lot of disinformation going around about $META “cutting capex” because they “overbuilt”.

This is an “if” they have excess capacity.

And it looks like the opposite right now:

Hyperscalers like $GOOGL are so compute constrained that they had to cut allocations to Meta back in March.

Since Meta was using too much for internal projects. 

Meta was immediately constrained so it looks like they were forced to immediately sign massive $48B+ contracts with Neoclouds like $CRWV and $NBIS.

Meta is selling excess capacity if there’s any, especially since their large contracts are take or pay from the Neoclouds. 

If anything, I’m expecting their guided capex to go up as they build out more independent capacity.

tweets.text:
The US Constitution was the most important political innovation ever, but it's missing two important things:

1) A cap on the growth of government spending
2) A requirement for hard-backed currency

Without them, every democracy drifts toward more debt and eventual loss of reserve currency status (see The Changing World Order). The US is $39T in debt, and adding $1T roughly every 100 days, with interest payments now exceeding the defense budget. There is no mechanism to stop it.

Politicians get elected by promising free stuff using other people's money. Some voters get a benefit today, while the negative impact of the spending goes to future generations who can't vote yet. The incentive structure is broken.

What fixes it? A new constitution somewhere on the frontier (Mars, special economic zones, cyberspace), an amendment that aligns incentives in the current system (politically challenging), or hyper economic growth (AI + robotics + crypto) to outpace inflation.

tweets.text:
Jonah and I have spent the last stretch building a research terminal, and today we're opening it up to everyone: https://t.co/eTvW9Kib7S

The origin of this is that we weren't trying to build a company we were trying to solve our own problem. We trade, and we were tired of the tools we had to trade with.

That framing changed everything about how we built it. Every feature had to earn its place by being something we actually reached for in a live session, not something that looked good in a demo.

If it didn't make our own research sharper, faster, or calmer, it didn't ship. We were the users we were building for, which meant we couldn't lie to ourselves about what was good enough.

For the last month, we've had it in the hands of our
@1000xPod group and have received great feedback, which has really helped us build out features that we think will irreplaceable.

We held off on a public launch until we were confident it was something we'd want in anyone's hands, not just ours. That point has arrived. So we're releasing it publicly, for everyone.

If you trade, we'd love for you to try it and tell us what you think. We're still building, and the best version of this comes from people using it and being honest with us about where it falls short.

tweets.text:
Robotics is crypto's most obvious blind spot.

In 2016 it wasn't even a category investors tracked. Now it's the 2nd biggest in private markets, ahead of fintech, at $263B. Q1 alone did $16B across almost 500 deals.

Here's what almost nobody in crypto is pricing in...all that capital is about to hit a wall the traditional financial stack can't get past.

A robot can't open a bank account. It can't hold a legal identity, sign a contract, or get paid through ACH. Every rail in TradFi assumes a human sits behind the transaction. That works when a person signs for a few thousand machines. It breaks when millions of them start transacting on their own.

Robotics routes through crypto because crypto is the only stack ever built for non-human actors:

• Permissionless identity a machine can hold without a passport or a corporation
• Programmable payments that settle machine to machine, no bank in the loop
• Machine-readable ownership, so an asset can belong to something that isn't a person
• Coordination between autonomous agents with no legal entity in the middle

You could always make the argument Robotics doesn't need crypto. 

But I've always focused on following where the world's attention and hype lies, and Crypto tends to find a way of monetizing that mindshare. 

Where I'm looking

Here's who's building those rails, grouped by the layer of the stack they're solving. 

Not a buy list btw...just a watchlist of which piece of the machine economy each one is going after.

• Coordination. Virtuals (@virtuals_io) runs the biggest AI agent ecosystem and is now pushing those agents into the physical world. Its Eastworld robotics track covers teleop data, simulation, and real robot deployment. 50k+ agents recently got access to 430+ tokenized equities through Ondo / Treasures.

• Location. Robots are useless without precise positioning, and GEODNET (@GEODNET) supplies it. 21k+ stations across 150+ countries, roughly $6.7M in annualized revenue and $5.3M in annualized holder revenue through buybacks and burns.

• Identity and payments. Fabric (@FabricFND) handles the backend: machine identity, wallets, payments, and task coordination. Plumbing for a world where machines transact onchain without a human in the loop.

• Telemetry. IoTeX (@iotex_io) has been grinding on DePIN and machine data for years. Device identity, verifiable offchain compute, real-world machine data. Not flashy, but it's what matters when robots need telemetry anyone can trust.

• Spatial. Auki (@AukiLabs) is building Posemesh, a spatial awareness network that helps robots, smart glasses, and other devices understand the physical spaces around them.

• Ownership. xMAQUINA (@xmaquina) wraps physical AI exposure in a DAO. Fractional machine ownership and robotics deals that normally sit behind VC rounds, backed by an $18M treasury with $7.6M set aside for robotics investments.

• Simulation. Strike (@StrikeRobot_ai) works on humanoid autonomy and sim-to-real training. They recently rebuilt a real Eastworlds lab inside a simulation to test whether behaviors trained in sim survive contact with the real world.

• Training data. Caspius (@caspius_ai) is after embodied AI data. Robots learn from real-world task footage, and Caspius just hit an ATH in footage contributed while adding NFT/account pairing.

• Tooling. Roba (@Roba_Labs) wants to make robotics easier to build around. No-code tools, simulation, robot templates, a creator marketplace, and compute for training and inference.

The catch

I'm not going to pretend this is a rotation you front-run next week. Most of these are early, thinly traded, and completely dependent on physical AI actually scaling. If robots stall, these rails don't matter yet.

But the capital is already moving, and it's moving toward a category that structurally needs crypto rails to work. I'd rather map this corner early than chase it once it's obvious.

• Ownership. xMAQUINA (@xmaquina) wraps physical AI exposure in a DAO. Fractional machine ownership and robotics deals that normally sit behind VC rounds, backed by an $18M treasury with $7.6M set aside for robotics investments.

• Simulation. Strike (@StrikeRobot_ai) works on humanoid autonomy and sim-to-real training. They recently rebuilt a real Eastworlds lab inside a simulation to test whether behaviors trained in sim survive contact with the real world.

• Training data. Caspius (@caspius_ai) is after embodied AI data. Robots learn from real-world task footage, and Caspius just hit an ATH in footage contributed while adding NFT/account pairing.

• Tooling. Roba (@Roba_Labs) wants to make robotics easier to build around. No-code tools, simulation, robot templates, a creator marketplace, and compute for training and inference.

The catch

I'm not going to pretend this is a rotation you front-run next week. Most of these are early, thinly traded, and completely dependent on physical AI actually scaling. If robots stall, these rails don't matter yet.

But the capital is already moving, and it's moving toward a category that structurally needs crypto rails to work. I'd rather map this corner early than chase it once it's obvious.

🚢Hormuz Vessel Crossing Update: Recovery Stalls at 19 — Tankers 10, Dry Cargo 9

Commercial vessel crossings through the Strait of Hormuz have slipped to 19 over the latest 24-hour rolling window — 10 tankers and 9 dry cargo ships — down from 24 yesterday and a full 67% below the June 24 recovery peak of 57. What looked like a rebuilding of traffic into late June has flattened into four straight readings stuck in the high-teens-to-20s, well short of anything resembling normal.

Breaking it out by vessel type tells the story of the stall. Both segments spiked on June 24 — 24 tankers and 33 dry cargo ships, each the strongest single-day count since the late-February collapse. But the dry-bulk dash has unwound fastest, off 73% from that peak versus 58% for tankers. The result: tankers have quietly reclaimed the slim majority of the flow (roughly 53%), closer to their pre-conflict share, but on a fraction of the volume.

Powered by our commercial vessel crossing index, using AIS data refreshed every 30 minutes.

tweets.text:
Citi: "Against this backdrop we update our forecasts, in the base case assuming no new [Bitcoin ETF] inflows in the next 12m. This reduces our central BTC forecast to $82k and for ETH to $2.2k. In our prior publications and research we have emphasized the outsized effects of ETF flows in explaining (the variation of) returns - Digital Asset Take: What’s Driving Bitcoin? (It’s Flows). 
The lack of legislative progress and negative sentiment in the sector has seen a reversal of flows YTD. Our targets were predicated on increased investor and financial advisor interest leading to gradual adoption and inflows from these traditional-financial sources. We think this process is likely put on pause until we see a catalyst. The average ETF holder is now underwater and prices are below their pre-2024 US election levels.
Legislation is one potential catalyst but seems unlikely before November’s US midterms. The return of “de-basement fears” could also increase investor interest but Warsh’s hawkish debut reduces those risks in the near-term in our view. Consequently, we reduce our flow assumption in the next 12m to zero in the base case and negative in the bear case. It is important to note that crypto markets are volatile, and sentiment driven which makes them reflexive in terms of price action, on-chain/off-chain activity, and flows. This increases forecast uncertainty but can cause abrupt changes in sentiment."

"And then along came blockchains."

Turing Award winner Barbara Liskov on how PBFT — practical Byzantine fault tolerance, now foundational to modern consensus — started as a PhD student answering a DARPA RFP:

"I had a student, Miguel Castro, who was looking for a PhD thesis, and I suggested to Miguel that he look at the RFPs that DARPA had put out … He found an RFP that was looking for ways to handle the malicious attacks that were going on on the internet."

"When Miguel and I finished the work on practical Byzantine fault tolerance, we thought that at some point people would start to use this. … It was a delay of about 10 years before people started to use it."

tweets.text:
RFQ based liquidations needs to be the future. All liquidations reviewed in the last week on @flyingtulip_ either closed the position at dollar for dollar parity or in some cases, the RFQ filled with positive price, meaning less debt seized. 

The standard liquidation flow is;

Position becomes liquidatable
Repay x amount of debt
Claim collateral

Because the above is not market aware it has to make some assumption around liquidation bonus to make it potentially attractive in high friction markets.

FT's flow is;

Position becomes liquidation.
Engine creates an RFQ offer to sell collateral for minOut debt.
Best quote fills

This means almost no haircut / liquidation penalty for the user, $ for $ repayment, and only the minimum amount to return to healthy.

If this was a normal trade and not RFQ, MEV Would likely fill at exactly minOut (which would have the same impact as liquidation bonus), however since RFQ can have multiple offer submissions, the protocol will always give the user the best fill. With this design your liquidation price is closer to a stoploss than position wipeout.

SATA and STRC are trying to strip out the volatility from a pref like STRF by adjusting the yield instead of the price, but the market is adjusting STRF yield every minute by moving its price, compared to the SATA yield which is adjusting much more slowly. This means that the volatility needs to "go somewhere" until you're adjusting the yield again, and given the volatility of STRF you should probably expect SATA to trade in a 20% range roughly, for example from $90 to $110. If you're absorbing all the upside volatility above $100, then you're most likely lowering the floor of the 20% range, so instead of having it trade between $90 and $110 you'll have it trade between $80 and $100

(On top of letting shorts have a free ride + cutting all upside above $100 for holders + selling at $100 something that people would be ready to buy at higher price)

All this to say that your decision is correct, but I think even under 'normal conditions' you shouldn't let the market expect a perma ATM at $100!

tweets.text:
You can't bluntly compare the dividend rate on prefs to the perp funding rate because the funding rate is proportional to the price of BTC whereas the dividend rate on prefs doesn't compound with the price of BTC. It's like if you locked in a funding rate at the price of BTC at year Y and then kept paying the total funding cost from year Y even on year Y+4 or even Y+10

In the short-term, they are very comparable, but in the long-term, they diverge significantly, which is precisely the mechanism that would enable outperformance vs BTC. Obviously, the whole thesis of Saylor is that BTC is going to compound at a high CAGR (20%+), so borrowing at a non-compounding 12% rate makes a lot of sense with that assumption in mind. But if you expect BTC to be flat for 5 years, then taking any leverage is by definition bad, whether it's 12% or 4% tbh

tweets.text:
The problem is that people want DAOs to democratise decision making where multiple disjoint groups are pivotable in the vote, including votes from people with no financial upside or stake in their decisions. 

DAOs, as currently implemented, do not enable the above goals. It is token weighted voting, where the goal is to encourage all large token holders to participate and vote. There might be some tokenholders significantly larger than others, but that’s how the system is intended to work. 

So the upset is arguably the mismatch between the vision (democratised voting) and the reality of weighted voting. 

The solution isn’t to attack a large tokenholder, especially since it is the founder of the project, for deciding to make governance more competitive and voting according to their own stake. 

Either DAOs need to design systems that do indeed democratise votes where no single actor can have the deciding vote. Or they should lean into the properties of token weighted voting and make voting a competitive landscape. 

We know the latter works. Nearly all large companies use weighted voting systems and they are worth trillions of dollars. There is a lot we can learn from the old legacy world.
